[02:58] off I want to explain that MTHFR is a methyl reductase enzyme. We are born with
[03:10] a gene that regulates, it's a couple of genes actually, we are born with genes
[03:15] that regulate our body's ability to create this methyl reductase enzyme or
[03:24] not okay. So that is why some people have this genetic variant and why some
[03:32] people do not. Some people are born with the genes that turn on the body's
[03:40] ability to make this reductase enzyme and some do not produce that enzyme at
[03:47] all. They're born with the genes turned off so there is no production of this
[03:53] enzyme. What does this enzyme do? Well remember that enzymes break apart if
[03:59] you remember way back into biology, I know I teach lots of biology don't I, if
[04:05] you think back to biology you remember that enzymes produce reactions and within
[04:14] the body many of those reactions are produced by an enzyme that cleaves or
[04:24] separates. If you use a meat cleaver what are you doing? You are chopping
[04:28] that meat into smaller pieces, that's what an enzyme does for us. So if you
[04:34] have a methyl reductase genetic variant you are unable to cleave or separate
[04:43] certain chemicals within the body. That process is a methylation defect so for
[04:50] the majority of people that have that enzyme they're able to cleave those
[04:57] ingredients, separate those nutrients, separate those chemicals, those hormones
[05:04] appropriately and let those processes go down their normal pathways. If you have a
[05:12] methyl reductase deficiency because your genetic makeup doesn't allow your
[05:18] body to produce this enzyme then the hormones, chemistry, the processes
[05:25] that are supposed to occur after that separation, after the scissors come
[05:30] along and clip those ingredients apart cannot occur. Okay so that's really what
[05:39] it boils down to. That's the simplest explanation of what this methyl
[05:44] tetrahydroxy reductase enzyme does, MTHFR. Okay so if you have this MTHFR gene it
[05:56] means your body has trouble methylating. Methylating is that process of
[06:03] separating molecules so that other processes continue with regard to those
[06:11] ingredients. Okay does everybody understand that so far? Methylation is a
[06:17] process of cleaving or separating nutrients molecules hormones during
[06:25] normal body processes to continue processes down that pathway. We're going
[06:30] to talk about the simplest version and then we're going to talk about a more
[06:34] complicated version. Okay so the simplest explanation that I give patients all the
[06:45] time with regard to MTHFR variants is the B12 molecule. Cyan cobalamin is the
[06:54] most common and least expensive B12 marketed today. When we take that
[07:01] particular kind of B12 cyan cobalamin it begins with a C like cyan cyanide blue
[07:07] like cyan. When we take cyan cobalamin into the body it is one molecule of two
[07:18] parts a cyanide molecule and a B12 or cobalamin molecule. Okay so it's combined
[07:26] chemically in the lab. When we take it and it starts going through digestion
[07:32] and absorption when we have the correct enzymes the MTHFR variant
[07:43] enzyme when we have the enzyme that enzyme comes along and cuts those two
[07:49] molecules apart allowing cyanide to be wasted and cobalamin then to have a
[07:55] place to park. Now it's activated it has places on here kind of like an
[08:04] electrical outlet you have a cord and you're gonna plug it in. You have your
[08:10] charger cord it when you're separating it from your brick it has a place to
[08:15] plug in. When you plug it into the brick it's no longer exposed so that's kind of
[08:19] what this process does. When cyanide is separated by methyl reductase enzyme
[08:27] cyanide gets wasted cobalamin gets used. Okay if you have the MTHFR variant you
[08:37] can't break this apart because you don't have that enzyme. Okay so you absorb you
[08:45] take this medicine in this B12 in but your body can't use it. So one of two
[08:51] things happens. Either it does get into your bloodstream but it can't go
[08:56] anywhere or it's completely wasted. There isn't any other place for this B12 to
[09:02] go. Sorry something must be in my coffee. Okay does everybody understand how this
[09:14] methyl reductase enzyme works? It works like scissors or a cleaver to separate
[09:22] molecules to go through processes. This is basic to where we're going because
[09:26] when I talk about thyroid it's going to get much more complicated. Okay this is
[09:32] why if you are interested in the MTHFR variant and or hypothyroidism related to
[09:39] MTHFR variants I would highly recommend you watch this over and over again
[09:45] because it's a pretty complicated topic. So methyl reductase is an enzyme that
[09:53] our body makes and it's designed to methylate or separate ingredients from
[10:00] one another so that body processes can occur. Alright when we talk about
[10:07] methylation processes a lot of people think that they cannot take methyl forms
[10:19] of B12 but it's exactly the opposite. If you have a methylation defect you need
[10:25] to take B vitamins that are already methylated. That means it doesn't have to
[10:30] go through the process of methylation. Your body can absorb it easily break it
[10:35] apart just with normal stomach acid and the process goes on. Okay so if you have
[10:41] a methylation defect 90% of people can use and should use methylated B's not
[10:49] cyanide based B vitamins. Okay so if you don't know your methylation status there
[10:58] is a genetic test available. Mainstream medicine does not typically like to do
[11:04] genetic testing unless there is some major reason to do so. A lot of times
[11:10] insurance companies will deny the coverage for genetic testing unless you
[11:15] can prove it's warranted. So I don't typically order the tests for these
[11:20] MTHFR variants unless you specifically ask and you are willing to pay that
[11:26] cost because a lot of times it's not covered by your insurer. If you're lucky
[11:31] enough to get it covered great that's awesome but most of the time genetic
[11:37] testing is not covered unless you can document specific reasoning. So the other
[11:45] few people I said 90% of the people with methylation defects can use
[11:49] methyl methylated vitamins. There's a few that can't. These are very rare. These
[11:55] have another genetic defect usually in addition to the MTHFR variant. These are
[12:03] a COMT genetic variant. If you have MTHFR and COMT variants then you typically
[12:11] have to find other forms of B12 if you need to use that supplement. Forms like
[12:18] adeno or hydroxy B12 are available. Sometimes they're only available from
[12:23] your prescriber as injectables but sometimes you can find them on a few of
[12:32] the more rare supplement sites. So you can find them. They're often a little
[12:41] pricier. So those are some things to think about with regard to the bees. But now
[12:47] let's dive into how this methyl reductase impacts thyroid care. So for about four
[12:55] or five years now I've had a practice of seeing lots and lots of hypothyroid
[13:01] patients. Lots of metabolic dysfunction. I would guess that probably 80 to 90% of
[13:09] my patients lined up with either temporary or permanent diagnoses of
[13:15] hypothyroidism. A lot of times it's Hashimoto's. A lot of times it's an
[13:21] ingredient deficiency like a nutrient you're not getting so your body can't
[13:26] produce enough of the hormone or it's a permanent failure or removal of the
[13:31] thyroid gland. So there are different causes of hypothyroidism. But one of
[13:36] the things I initially found quite interesting and continue to find it
[13:42] interesting this article helps explain it and I'll post a link to this article

[14:03] cell membrane or in the circulating blood just outside the cell. And it's
[14:10] important for us to understand because I did not in the beginning how MTHFR is
[14:19] impacting thyroid health. I found it quite interesting that many of my
[14:27] patients with hypothyroidism especially those with Hashimoto's were found to
[14:35] also have genetically positive tested MTHFR variants. So I'm like hmm this is
[14:45] interesting. This patient this patient this patient all these people they've

[15:17] positive for the MTHFR variant and most of these patients also had Hashimoto's
[15:22] autoimmune hypothyroidism. So I'm thinking there's got to be a connection
[15:27] here and it's an interesting connection because number one mainstream medicine
[15:39] really ignores this whole process with regard to T3 formation. So when you
[15:49] really look at the chemical processes that are creating free T3 using the
[15:57] enzymes that the body has to do this it really becomes remarkably intriguing. So
[16:06] and the graphic that I have from this article is quite interesting because it
[16:12] shows T4 coming into the cell and T3 being used. So when we have this process
[16:23] going on methylation is required for these other thyroid hormones and enzymes
[16:32] to work. So first off let's talk about some basic definitions. So free T3 is the
[16:41] only active molecule of your thyroid hormone function that can be utilized to
[16:48] produce energy. T4 cannot be used, TSH is not used, reverse T3 is not used,
[16:58] your Hashimoto's antibodies are not used, the TPO or thyroglobulin antibodies
[17:05] they are not used any sort of in any sort of process related to free T3
[17:11] utilization. They're completely different. Free T3 is the only thyroid hormone
[17:18] that enters the cell for ATP or energy production. Okay keep that thought in
[17:25] mind as we go through this discussion. Okay free T4 is the precursor to free
[17:33] T3. That's why most of the mainstream treatment plans prescribe levothyroxine
[17:42] or synthroid or similar products. That is a T4 product. The basic understanding is
[17:51] that that T4 product goes into your body is absorbed and utilized and sent to
[17:58] the cell and therefore your enzymes are supposed to come along and convert that
[18:04] T4 into T3. Those enzymes take away an iodine molecule. Okay when the body and
[18:12] genes are functioning properly that's how T3 is made. But I'm finding that
[18:20] loads and loads of people with hypothyroidism are unable to convert that
[18:25] T4 to T3 properly. Okay there's a lot of reasons for that. I don't know them all.
[18:32] I'm only investigating learning and studying sometimes right alongside you.
[18:36] Okay but if your body can't convert T4 into free T3 you become seriously
[18:46] hypothyroid and symptoms continue to worsen the lower that T3 level gets.
[18:52] Okay all right so free T3 is the only energy producing thyroid hormone. T4 must
[19:00] become free T3 using enzymatic processes and methylation. Okay so if you're
[19:11] looking at the picture once I post that for y'all you'll see that there are some
[19:20] enzymes on the cell membrane. If you think about the cell membrane go back to
[19:26] biology maybe Google an image of the cell membrane. You remember that there's
[19:31] a phospholipid bilayer. Okay there's two layers to the outside of the cell
[19:37] and all around that phospholipid bilayer are various receptors and enzymes waiting
[19:45] for nearby chemistry to come along and act on that cell. They're just parked
[19:51] there just like your cars parked in a parking lot. They're just waiting for
[19:55] something to happen. Okay when T4 comes along these enzymes become activated and
[20:06] it says oh here's some T4 if I grab onto that I can bring that in the cell
[20:12] and I can convert it to T3 and this person is going to get some energy from
[20:18] this conversion. It's not that simple but that's the basics. Okay if your body
[20:26] does not make those enzymes what happens to that T4 molecule? Does it ever become
[20:34] free T3? Does it? If you don't make the enzymes to separate that iodine molecule
[20:50] from free T4 to make free T3 will you ever have better T3? No of course not.
[21:05] Your T4 level is going to continue to rise and as it rises something else
[21:14] happens. One of those enzymes we're going to specify them here in a minute but
[21:19] one of those enzymes says you've been around here a long time you're not doing
[21:23] anything I'm going to cut you off and so it takes away one of those T4 iodine
[21:31] molecules and the way it removes it makes that molecule become a reverse T3
[21:39] molecule. It's a waste product. It is not useful. The body cannot use reverse
[21:47] T3. It's a different type of molecule in shape and connectivity kind of like you
[21:57] ever pull into one of those big garage car garages or big parking lots and
[22:04] you're driving around looking for a spot and you can't find anything you
[22:08] finally oh there's one right up there and you get there and it says for
[22:11] compact cars only and your big old SUV will not fit in that hole. It's kind of
[22:17] the same thing. Okay reverse T3 is not similar to free T3 because of its
[22:25] connectivity. It doesn't have the right plug-in. It's not gonna fit in the
[22:30] parking space. Okay another example if you've ever lived in Europe, England,
[22:38] other countries, instead of the US, your outlets don't always look like this, right?
[22:46] Can you take this plug-in and put in one of those outlets and have your item work
[22:52] properly? No. When you travel internationally they usually tell you
[22:59] bring an adapter, bring this kind of adapter for your electronic devices
[23:03] because they're not gonna work. The plug-in is different. It's the same
[23:07] with free T3 compared to reverse T3. The plug-in will not work. So reverse T3
[23:14] cannot be used for energy production. It won't fit in the receptor in the cell.
[23:20] Okay all right so there are three of these di adenase enzymes. There's DIO1,
[23:30] DIO2, and DIO3 and it's easier for me to say DIO1, 2, and 3 than it is
[23:36] the adenase every time I say that. Okay so we're using initials. Okay but those are
[23:43] specific enzymes that come along and manage T4 production and utilization. So
[23:53] DIO1 and DIO2 are responsible for creating free T3. So T4 comes along,
[24:04] floats by the cell membrane. The DIO1 or 2 says, oh you belong here, pull in
[24:11] here. Like we got a place for you to park. Come on, come on, come on over here. And
[24:15] so those two enzymes pull the T4 toward them with electrical charges and the
[24:23] process begins. They begin to cleave or separate one iodine molecule away from
[24:30] that T4 molecule and it's put into circulation to go back and find another
[24:36] place to work. Iodine is recirculated in the system quite nicely. But if you have
[24:45] an MTHFR variant, those enzymes may not be cleaving at all or they may be
[24:56] cleaving inappropriately. So or they may just be cleaving part-time if you because
[25:03] you have various genetic influences. One allele or two alleles on a gene indicate
[25:12] how much of an impact that enzyme might have. Okay so for instance why do some
[25:22] people have hair color or eye color that's mixed? It's because they have
[25:28] alleles from different genes from different parents. Okay so alleles are
[25:34] the tiniest forms of genetic material that guide manifestation of a
[25:39] characteristic. Blue hair, blue eyes, green eyes or brown hair, blonde hair. Okay so
[25:46] those guide what is going to happen down the line with processes with
[25:51] relationship to the genes and enzymes. So if you have a methylation defect, one of
[25:58] the things that does not happen appropriately is free T4 becoming free
[26:04] T3. It's just not gonna happen. It's just not. Methylation, MTHFR variants will
[26:14] impact the body's ability to methylate or separate that free T4 to free T3.
[26:21] Okay so that's what happens when people do not make methyl reductase enzymes. Okay
[26:32] those enzymes are vital to free T3 formation for normal thyroid function.
[26:39] Okay questions about any of that? I know it's pretty complicated but it's really
[26:50] helpful for people with MTHFR variants to understand why their body is not
[26:56] producing free T3 appropriately. It's why I typically encourage patients worldwide
[27:04] to get a free T3 level, a reverse T3 level because if your body cannot do
[27:10] this appropriately, you develop hypothyroidism and you have terrible
[27:16] thyroid symptoms way before the level falls low enough to be seen on a blood
[27:22] test. So when we talk about free T3 levels ideal even not on meds is about
[27:28] 3.4 or 3.5. If you are not on thyroid medication and your free T3 is 2.8
[27:38] that is in normal range. Normal is down to about 2.1 or 2.3 depending on
[27:45] the lab you're using. But just above 2 is considered normal but at 2.3 or 4 or 5
[27:54] or 6 or 8 most patients are extremely symptomatic. Terrible fatigue, terrible
[28:04] brain fog, terrible gut motility, terrible skin and hair health like all of
[28:11] the systems are impacted because energy is used by all parts of your body. It's
[28:16] not just for your thyroid health, it's for every single cell in your body. Without
[28:23] adequate T3 you're not going to grow healthy hair, you're not going to have
[28:27] healthy skin, your skin is going to feel and look dry and dull and unhealthy. Okay
[28:34] so to get healthy skin you want to optimize your thyroid, you want to
[28:38] optimize your vitamin D, you want to optimize all of the ingredients that are
[28:44] needed to produce healthy skin. The same goes for healthy thyroid. If you are not
[28:49] consuming the appropriate ingredients vitamin D, vitamin K2, iodine and protein
[28:59] and iron you're not your body can't make enough thyroid hormone to function.
[29:03] Okay so it's really important that you get all of your thyroid tested if you
[29:09] want your thyroid looked at. Getting a TSH is pretty useless. That's a thyroid
[29:15] stimulating hormone that's released from the pituitary gland. It is not useful by
[29:21] the thyroid gland except to kick the thyroid and say hey bud I need some
[29:26] more T4 out here. It doesn't do anything else for your health. There is
[29:31] absolutely no benefit to testing a TSH if you're not going to test the rest of
[29:37] the numbers. If your provider refuses to test the rest of the numbers find
[29:43] another provider. I'm just going to be that bold and blunt. Find someone who
[29:48] will test all six of yours at least once. All six thyroid numbers TSH, free T4,
[29:57] free T3, reverse T3 and then the two Hashimoto's antibodies TPO and
[30:03] thyroid globulin antibody. Okay if you think because of various issues that have
[30:11] come up over time or if you have a really high homocysteine level or really
[30:17] high B12 level that you don't understand yet you might still have
[30:22] symptoms of low B12. Low symptoms of low B12 are associated with
[30:28] pernicious anemia. You can have those blood tests done and if you think you
[30:32] have those impacts that might help you justify getting an MTHFR genetic test
[30:39] done especially if they're causing symptoms. So it's something to consider and
[30:45] to think about. Okay when you think you have a thyroid problem it's very
[30:51] important to evaluate all six of those but it's also important to evaluate the

[31:01] K and then you need your iron, iodine and protein. If you don't know where those
[31:06] are coming from you should check your diet, check your supplements, look into why
[31:11] you're not getting those ingredients because those are ingredients vital for

[33:50] and everywhere you stop you're picking up people and letting off people. You're
[33:55] driving around town you're just driving and doing your job. However when when
[34:01] you get to certain locations for some reason the gate won't let you in if you
[34:10] have more than enough people on the bus that they allow. Let's say it's five. They
[34:15] don't want more than five people on the bus coming through their gate today. So
[34:20] you're driving around and you're picking up and letting off people and you're
[34:23] picking up and letting off people. That's just like ingredients coming in and out
[34:28] of the cell. Okay the bus is the cell. Okay so you're getting in and out of the
[34:33] cell and all this stuff but now now you want to get through this gate to get
[34:39] more people. You have a project or something on the other side of there but
[34:45] the gate master won't let the cell through won't let the items through
[34:51] without specific characteristics or people in your bus. So then you other
[34:59] people come along and try to persuade people off the bus or put people on the
[35:04] bus to get your magic number. That's what's happening with chemistry in the
[35:09] cell. As the cell is floating around in your body in your bloodstream sitting in
[35:17] tissues, in the liver tissue, in the heart tissue. As cells sit there they have
[35:23] very specific tasks and as ingredients come by they're either persuaded in or
[35:30] pushed out based on what their project is for that moment. Okay it's kind of
[35:37] like a bus route. So the bus is going around picking up and letting off but
[35:41] certain things can't happen if there aren't the right ingredients in the

[36:57] of the process isn't working for you then you have symptoms and that's how

[37:38] every single cell. Okay so methylation is impacting our health whether it the
[37:47] body does it right or it does it not so well or completely it doesn't do it at
[37:53] all. So like methylation impacts health no matter what if you can methylate
[37:59] great. If you can't then you have real problems but both yes it's methylating

[38:55] sites I think one of them is geneticgenie or geneticcode.com something like that
[39:00] you can load your data into those sites you can't download a report without
[39:05] paying for it but you can see the report online you can upload your data I
[39:10] think it's geneticgenie.com it's one of them it's a free site to look at but
[39:16] the report does cost if you want to download and print it if you want to
[39:20] screenshot it you can screenshot and screenshot and screenshot all different
[39:24] sections I guess some sites prevent that I don't know about that site but yeah if
[39:30] you buy the basic product Diane you're not gonna get the medical breakdown you
[39:35] have to ask for that medical report and that's the only way to get it. Let's see
[39:43] Linda says if you have MTHFR deficiency is it worth being on T4 yes because T4
[39:50] is going to become reverse T3 and reverse T3 acts a little bit like brakes
[39:57] so you have to balance the same way that you use your gas pedal and your brake
[40:02] pedal to drive to the store your body is using gas pedals and brake pedals to
[40:07] drive your energy and fatigue like you're supposed to have energy in the daytime
[40:12] you're supposed to get tired at night and so free T3 helps with energy in the
[40:17] daytime reverse T3 helps with that circadian rhythm that provides nighttime
[40:21] so yes you still have to have some T4 but it's not going to be dose the way
[40:27] mainstream doses it like in the 100 to 150 175 microgram doses it's going to be
[40:33] 25 or 50 usually sometimes a little more than that but T4 is vital to keep
[40:40] reverse T3 in check in some patients some patients don't need T4 dosing at
[40:45] all it's not helping and so we just don't use it mainstream continues to use
[40:53] it no matter what so yeah they're learning here and there a few
